behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is at real git-change detection plus CLI/report wiring, not just local categorization logic. A mock diff can hide real rename semantics (e.g., when git reports rename vs add+delete based on similarity/index state). REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both apply because this behavior depends on actual repository/index behavior and typically real git CLI/subprocess behavior. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text requires true rename handling in the repository being analyzed; fake module handoff cannot guarantee that. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML output dependency, no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp git repo, commit a file, rename it, and run the real CLI against that repo (as a subprocess) for both staged and committed rename scenarios. Assert stdout report has the file only in `renamed`, and that `added`/`deleted` do not include that same path pair.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary diff handling failures usually appear when parsing real git output and real file contents (including non-text/binary markers), so in-process fakes can miss crashes or parse errors. REAL-INFRA applies due real repository/files and likely git subprocess I/O; MOCK-HIDE applies because mocked text responses can omit edge byte patterns. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior explicitly references binary diffs from the repository/tool runtime path, which is exactly where real infra failures surface. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified: no model-dependent correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp repo with at least one binary file change and one text change, run the real CLI, and assert process exit is successful, report is emitted, and binary-changed file is counted in the correct category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This edge case commonly depends on real git command/revision behavior in an unborn-HEAD repo (e.g., non-zero exit paths), which mocks often flatten. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE apply because handling actual repo state and command errors is the core risk. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text is specifically about running the tool on a real no-commit repository, where subprocess/OS/git behavior is the failure source. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by lack of AI/ML steps.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new temp git repo with zero commits, invoke the CLI, assert zero exit or defined success behavior, and verify stdout is a valid empty structured report (not an exception trace).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is end-to-end correctness against real git history, requiring actual commit graph/diff behavior and full CLI output path. A mocked repository API can pass while diverging from real git semantics, so MOCK-HIDE applies. REAL-INFRA applies because the behavior is tied to real repository history and typically command-line/runtime interactions. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text explicitly anchors truth to the repository’s actual history, which requires real infra execution. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified: no LLM/model decision quality involved.
behaviors.test_description: Build a temp repo with a known sequence of commits that includes add/delete/modify/rename events, run the CLI over the target history window, and compare each reported count to expected values derived from explicit git operations performed in setup.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Staged vs unstaged equivalence depends on real index/worktree behavior and git status/diff plumbing, which frequently diverges from mocks. REAL-INFRA applies because OS filesystem + git index state are central, and MOCK-HIDE applies because fake status providers can trivially return identical data and miss real mismatch bugs. Rejection-gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text explicitly compares two real repository states (index vs working tree), which is a system boundary. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified: no AI/ML dependency or multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: In a temp repo, make identical file edits in two scenarios: (a) staged and (b) unstaged. Run the real CLI in each scenario and assert the structured stdout payloads are identical.
